title: Stage 1 — The Basing Phase
subtitle: Patience is the only skill required here

lessons.title: Chart Characteristics
lessons.visual_caption: Stage 1: Price moves sideways inside a horizontal range. The 30-week SMA goes flat.
lessons.content:
Stage 1 is the basing phase. After a long decline, the stock stops falling and enters a period of quiet consolidation.

Trend Direction: A sideways trend. The stock stops making lower lows and lower highs.

Price Boundaries: Price bounces inside a narrow horizontal channel — a resistance ceiling on top, a support floor below. It touches the ceiling, turns back. Touches the floor, bounces. Over and over.

The Master Line: The 30-week SMA loses its downward slope and goes flat. Price drifts closely above and below it. The line no longer tells you direction — it just sits there horizontally.

Volume: Very low and quiet — public interest is effectively dead. There is nothing exciting happening and nobody is talking about the stock.

→ Sideways price inside a horizontal range
→ 30-week SMA is flat and horizontal
→ Volume is low and quiet
→ Stock is being ignored by the public

lessons.title: Market Psychology in Stage 1
lessons.visual_caption: Amber Enterprises — Look at the long quiet period before the explosive move. That quiet period is Stage 1 accumulation happening in silence.
lessons.content:
Understanding what is actually happening during Stage 1 changes how you see a boring, sideways stock.

Balance of Power: Buyers and sellers are evenly matched. Neither controls direction. That is why the stock goes sideways.

The Big Investors: Institutions are quietly accumulating shares over many months. They buy slowly so they do not push the price up — they do not want anyone to notice. This is called accumulation.

The Public: Retail investors ignore the stock. Nothing exciting is happening. The news is quiet or still negative from the prior Stage 4 decline. Nobody wants to talk about it.

The Amber chart above shows this perfectly. Look at the long, quiet, low-volume period before the explosive advance. Nothing was happening. Most investors ignored it. But institutions were quietly loading up.

This is the cruel irony of Stage 1: the best time to notice a stock is when it is the most boring, and most people are not watching it.

→ Institutions accumulate quietly
→ Retail ignores the stock
→ Boring = healthy base building
→ The best setups come from the most ignored stocks

lessons.title: Action Rules — What To Do in Stage 1
lessons.content:
Stage 1 has one rule: sit idle. Do not buy yet.

The Master Rule: Do not buy a Stage 1 stock.

The Reason: A stock can stay in Stage 1 for months or years. Buying now traps your capital and costs you opportunities elsewhere. You might wait 18 months for nothing to happen while other Stage 2 stocks double.

The Discipline: Wait patiently for a confirmed breakout — covered in the next module — before committing any money.

What you CAN do in Stage 1:
→ Add the stock to your watchlist
→ Set a price alert at the resistance ceiling level
→ Monitor it weekly — is volume starting to pick up?
→ Is the 30-week SMA beginning to curve upward?

When to get excited about a Stage 1 stock:
The transition from Stage 1 to Stage 2 is the most important signal in the entire method. Watch for: price approaching the resistance ceiling, volume picking up on up-weeks, the 30-week SMA beginning to slope upward.

But until that breakout happens on high volume — sit idle.

→ Never buy in Stage 1
→ Add to watchlist and monitor
→ Capital trapped in Stage 1 cannot work elsewhere
→ Wait for the confirmed high-volume breakout

questions.question: What is the primary direction of price during Stage 1?
questions.explanation: Stage 1 is a sideways base — price moves flat inside a horizontal range while accumulation takes place quietly.

questions.question: What does the 30-week SMA look like in Stage 1?
options: It curves up and down each day
questions.explanation: Because average price is stable, the 30-week line flattens out and runs horizontally. A flat 30-week SMA is one of the key identifiers of Stage 1.

questions.question: Why does Weinstein tell students to sit idle in Stage 1?
options: The stock is guaranteed to crash to zero
Institutions are selling all their shares
Capital can stay trapped in a flat stock for months or years
You should only buy when volume is falling
questions.explanation: Buying in Stage 1 carries a major opportunity cost — your money sits still instead of working in a stock that is already in Stage 2 and moving.

questions.question: What are institutions doing during the Stage 1 basing phase?
options: Selling all their shares as fast as possible
Quietly accumulating shares slowly so they do not move the price
Waiting for the stock to fall into Stage 4
Ignoring the stock completely like everyone else
questions.explanation: Institutions accumulate quietly over many months during Stage 1, buying slowly enough to avoid alerting the public. This is why Stage 1 can last so long — and why the breakout is so explosive when it finally comes.
